metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the tolerant-reader framing: explicitly state that omitting the `signature` field is a *breaking* change for un-upgraded reducers, not an additive one, and document that the upgrade-order constraint (reconcile hosts first) is a hard prerequisite — not a nice-to-have — before any new op-cert SIGNATURE events are written.
findings.finding: The design's tolerant-reader / 'old clients ignore the unknown field' claim is contradicted by the fact that new op-cert SIGNATURE events carry NO HMAC `signature` field — they omit an existing field rather than adding one. The git gpgsig analogy only holds for added fields. Old `process_signature` code accessing `record['signature']` (or equivalent) would fail, not silently ignore, making the additive-compatibility claim in the design section contradict the 'NO HMAC signature' spec in the same section and the rollout's preserve-and-ignore assertion.
findings.location: Selected design — Option A / Rollout paragraph; AC #4
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'older clones preserve-and-ignore' with: 'New op-cert SIGNATURE events omit the legacy `signature` field; un-upgraded reducers WILL fail on these events. The upgrade-order constraint (all reconcile hosts upgraded before first op-cert event is written) is therefore a hard deployment prerequisite, not an optional rollout note. Document this in the rollout section and enforce it via a deployment gate.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the tolerant-reader framing: clarify that omitting the HMAC `signature` field means old `verify_record` returns 'unsigned' (not a silent ignore), and promote the reconcile-host upgrade ordering from a parenthetical note to an explicit hard prerequisite ('MUST upgrade reconcile hosts before any op-cert SIGNATURE events are written').
findings.finding: The plan frames the op-cert approach as 'tolerant-reader: old clients ignore the unknown field' and compares it to the git gpgsig additive header pattern. But op-cert SIGNATURE events *omit* the existing `signature` field rather than merely adding new fields. Omitting a previously-present field is not additive — old `process_signature` will store `record['signature'] = None`, and old `verify_record` will return `verdict='unsigned'` for those events. The git gpgsig analogy only holds for purely additive changes. The plan's rollout guard ('upgrade reconcile hosts first') is the real mitigation but is framed as a nice-to-have note rather than a hard prerequisite.
findings.location: Selected design — Option A, Rollout paragraph; also AC #4 ('older clones preserve-and-ignore')
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'older clones preserve-and-ignore' with: 'Un-upgraded reducers will store the op-cert record with signature=None and verify_record will return unsigned — which is safe (no false certify) but misleading. The reconcile-host upgrade MUST precede any op-cert production; this is a hard ordering constraint, not a nice-to-have.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the rollout-tolerance rationale: SIGNATURE is in KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES so old `process_signature` actively drops unknown fields from reduced state (unlike TAG_DELTA which is skipped entirely by old reducers); document that the ordering constraint is stronger — no compaction may occur on an old reducer after op-cert events are emitted, and old clients see incomplete `attestations[kind]` records (missing envelope) until upgraded.
findings.finding: The plan invokes 'the TAG_DELTA precedent' to justify that 'older clones preserve-and-ignore' new SIGNATURE event fields — but TAG_DELTA is a new/unknown event type (old reducers skip unknown event types entirely), while SIGNATURE is in KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES (_version.py:109). Old reducers actively reduce SIGNATURE events via the existing fixed-field process_signature, which silently drops any unknown keys like envelope/material_fingerprint/merged_log_commit from the reduced attestations[kind] record. The raw event file is preserved, but the reduced state on old clients loses the new fields — making the rollout tolerance claim misleading for the state layer.
findings.location: Selected design — Option A, Rollout paragraph: 'the new fields are additive and preserved-and-ignored by older clones … mirroring the TAG_DELTA precedent'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'mirroring the TAG_DELTA precedent' with an accurate description: 'Old clients process SIGNATURE events via the existing fixed-field process_signature, dropping unknown fields from the reduced attestations[kind] record. The raw event files are preserved. Upgrading reducers (reconcile hosts) before any op-cert producer runs ensures no compaction strips op-cert fields from a snapshot; child consumers (4214) must also be upgraded before they attempt to read op-cert records.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `verify_record` / `verify_signature` behaves when it encounters an op-cert record (algorithm='sshsig', no `signature` field): either add a dispatch branch that routes to op-cert verification, or document explicitly that these callers return 'unsigned' for op-cert records and that op-cert verification is ONLY done via `opcert.opcert_from_record` + `opcert.verify_opcert` — and that the close gate / gating callers must be updated to handle this new path.
findings.finding: The op-cert SIGNATURE event carries NO HMAC `signature` field, but the existing `verify_record()` (signing.py:365) checks `stored_sig = record.get('signature') or ''` and returns `verdict='unsigned'` when it is absent. Any call to `verify_signature()` on a ticket whose most-recent or kind-keyed SIGNATURE event is an op-cert record will silently return 'unsigned' instead of routing to op-cert verification. The plan does not specify how existing HMAC-path callers should behave when they encounter an op-cert record — the data-flow is incomplete.
findings.location: Selected design — Option A, Producer bullet ('NO HMAC signature'); also affects AC #1 and Success Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: In the Producer/Reducer design section, add: 'Existing `verify_record` callers will return `unsigned` for op-cert records (no HMAC `signature` field). Callers that need to gate on an op-cert must use `opcert_from_record` + `verify_opcert` directly. Document this as an explicit non-interop: HMAC-verify and op-cert-verify are separate code paths.' If HMAC-path misfire is not acceptable, add a branch: `if record.get('algorithm') == 'sshsig': return {... 'verdict': 'not_applicable', ...}`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a rollback section asserting the schema STATE after rollback (not just 'rollback exits 0'): document that post-upgrade SNAPSHOTs carry op-cert fields in compiled_state, that a code rollback must be paired with a compaction freeze or compensating forward-migration, and attach a DATA LOSS RISK note covering the scenario where old process_signature drops envelope on next compaction.
findings.finding: The plan does not assert the schema STATE of reduced ticket state after a rollback of the reducer change: SNAPSHOTs written post-upgrade embed envelope/material_fingerprint/merged_log_commit inside attestations[kind] records in compiled_state; a rolled-back (old) process_signature rebuilds records from a fixed field set that excludes those fields, so the NEXT compaction after rollback silently drops op-cert fields — leaving op-cert records unverifiable without any error. This is a potential DATA LOSS RISK on partial failure/rollback.
findings.location: Selected design — Rollout section; AC line 4 ('Rollout note documented'); process_signature record construction lines 585-597
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Rollout section: 'Rollback safety: if the reducer change must be reverted after SNAPSHOTs have been written, do NOT run compaction under the old code — old process_signature will rebuild attestations records without envelope/material_fingerprint/merged_log_commit, silently purging op-cert data from compiled_state. Safe back-out path: (1) freeze compaction, (2) revert code, (3) treat any SNAPSHOT written post-upgrade as authoritative (restore verbatim via process_snapshot), OR compensate-forward by keeping the new reducer pinned and reverting only the producer. DATA LOSS RISK: compaction under old code after envelope-bearing SNAPSHOTs exist will permanently drop op-cert fields.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact manifest[0] line that `sign_opcert_manifest` must write (e.g. `"rebar.opcert.v1: <material_fingerprint>"`) so `attestation_kind` produces `OPCERT_KIND` and the record lands under the correct key.
findings.finding: The manifest[0] content that sign_opcert_manifest must place in the SIGNATURE event is never specified. The attestation_kind function keys attestations[kind] by the substring before ':' in manifest[0]. If sign_opcert_manifest omits manifest or uses a line that does not produce 'rebar.opcert.v1' before the colon, the reduced record lands under the wrong key or is not filed at all.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Producer bullet, replace '<kind>: …' with the concrete value: e.g. manifest[0] = f"{opcert.OPCERT_KIND}: {material_fingerprint}". This ensures attestation_kind returns 'rebar.opcert.v1' and child 4214 finds the record at attestations['rebar.opcert.v1'].

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate which export layers `sign_opcert_manifest` must reach (`signing.py` only, `_lib_writes.py`, `rebar/__init__.__all__`) and add those files to the edit set.
findings.finding: `sign_opcert_manifest` is placed in `signing.py` parallel to `sign_manifest`, but the plan never specifies whether it must also be plumbed through `rebar._lib_writes.sign_manifest`'s counterpart and exported in `rebar/__init__.__all__`. Children ee0b/8f1d import from the public `rebar` namespace; `sign_manifest` is exposed at three layers (`signing.py`, `_lib_writes.py`, `rebar/__init__.py`/__all__`), and the plan is silent on which layers `sign_opcert_manifest` must reach.
findings.location: Selected design — Producer bullet; src/rebar/_lib_writes.py:624; src/rebar/__init__.py:192

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback procedure for the reducer change: either gate new field preservation behind a feature flag (so the old path is one config flip away), or document that a rollback requires full ticket re-replay and estimate/bound that cost so operators are not surprised.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no explicit rollback procedure for the reducer change. Rolling back `process_signature` (e.g., if a bug surfaces post-deploy) requires re-replaying all tickets without the new field preservation logic; the plan does not acknowledge this cost or provide a mitigation (e.g., a feature flag gating the new field preservation, or a note that the reducer change is safe to revert because the new fields are simply absent from older snapshots).
findings.location: Selected design — Reducer bullet; AC line 2 (compact-then-replay round-trip)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line feature flag (e.g., `OPCERT_FIELDS_ENABLED = env_bool('OPCERT_FIELDS', default=True)`) read inside `process_signature`; setting it False instantly restores old reducer behavior on the next replay, making rollback a config change with zero data cleanup.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan covering the irreversible case: e.g., a forward-fix reducer patch that tolerates both old and new SIGNATURE event shapes, or a documented decision that rollback is not possible once new events are written (with a feature-flag gate as the guard).
findings.finding: The plan lacks an explicit rollback/back-out plan for the irreversible step of writing envelope-bearing SIGNATURE events (without an HMAC `signature` field) to an append-only ticket log. 'Upgrade reconcile hosts first' is an ordering constraint, not a rollback mechanism; once new events are written, old reducer code cannot process them.
findings.location: Selected design — Option A / Rollout paragraph

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Provide the complete typed signatures for `sign_opcert_manifest` and `opcert_from_record` in the plan (parameters, types, return types), and fix the set-literal notation `{material_fingerprint, merged_log_commit}` to dict notation.
findings.finding: sign_opcert_manifest and opcert_from_record are referenced as deliverables throughout the plan and ACs, but neither exists in the codebase (grep of src/ returns no matches) and neither function's public parameter list is fully specified in the plan. sign_opcert_manifest's caller-facing signature (what ee0b/8f1d must pass) is only implied; opcert_from_record's return type uses set-literal notation ({material_fingerprint, merged_log_commit}) that is ambiguous between a set and a dict.
findings.location: Selected design — Producer bullet; Read helper bullet; AC1; AC3; Success Criteria

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md entry capturing the Option A decision, the additive-field/tolerant-reader invariant, and the record schema (envelope + material_fingerprint + merged_log_commit fields) so the contract is discoverable by implementers of 4214/ee0b/8f1d.
findings.finding: The architectural decision (Option A vs B, tolerant-reader schema evolution pattern, keystone contract for 3 child tickets) is documented only in the ticket body, not in an ADR or CLAUDE.md update. Future contributors working on 4214/ee0b/8f1d will not have a durable, discoverable record of why this pattern was chosen or how the record schema is expected to evolve.
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/opcert-signature-event-extension.md` (or add a section to CLAUDE.md under 'Attestation schema') documenting: (1) the chosen pattern, (2) the fields added, (3) the tolerant-reader invariant, (4) why Option B was rejected, (5) the rollout ordering requirement.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify idempotency behavior for `sign_opcert_manifest`: either (a) check for an existing op-cert attestation of the same kind before appending and no-op/overwrite, or (b) explicitly document that duplicate events are safe and why (last-write-wins in reducer is sufficient).
findings.finding: The plan does not specify idempotency behavior for `sign_opcert_manifest`: if called twice for the same ticket+kind, two SIGNATURE events are appended, producing two `attestations[kind]` entries (the later one wins in the reducer, but the duplicate event persists in the event log and survives compaction as an extra replay step). Child tickets 4214/ee0b/8f1d may invoke the producer in retry loops with no guard.
findings.location: AC 1 / Producer section ('sign_opcert_manifest writes an envelope-bearing SIGNATURE event')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] For each of the four ACs, add a concrete proving command (e.g. `pytest tests/signing/test_sign_opcert_manifest.py -k test_<name>`) that an executor can copy-paste to produce machine-readable evidence of success.
findings.finding: No concrete proving commands (e.g. `pytest tests/...::test_name`) are specified for any AC; each AC says only 'a test asserts X', leaving the executor to discover or invent the test file paths and invocations, and leaving reviewers with no deterministic command to reproduce done-ness.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — all four AC bullets
findings.suggested_fix:
Append a `Proving commands` block after the ACs, e.g.:
```
# AC1
pytest tests/signing/test_sign_opcert_manifest.py -k test_opcert_record_fields -v
# AC2
pytest tests/reducer/test_process_signature.py -k test_compaction_round_trip -v
# AC3
pytest tests/opcert/test_opcert_from_record.py -k "test_opcert_record or test_hmac_record or test_end_to_end_verify" -v
# AC4
pytest tests/signing/test_sign_manifest.py -k test_legacy_record_unchanged -v
```
File paths are illustrative; replace with actual paths once test files are created.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add error handling specification to `sign_opcert_manifest`: define which exceptions are caught/wrapped into a structured error (e.g., `OpCertSigningError`), what is logged at failure (key_path, kind, ticket_id), and confirm the event-append is atomic with respect to the envelope build step.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no error handling or observability for signing failures in `sign_opcert_manifest` (e.g., missing/malformed key at key_path, DSSE signing error, event-append failure). As a shared primitive invoked by three child tickets in CI pipelines, silent failures or unstructured exceptions would be hard to debug.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `opcert_from_record` has ≥2 additional callers beyond 4214 (e.g., ee0b's server code and 8f1d's local-produce path both call it), OR inline its logic into 4214's gate as `dsse.decode(record['envelope'])` + field reads to avoid a single-caller wrapper in the public `opcert` module surface.
findings.finding: `opcert_from_record` is a new abstraction with only one confirmed call-site (the 4214 merge-gate verifier); its body is thin enough to inline as `dsse.decode(record['envelope'])` + two field reads using `dsse.decode`, which already has 5+ call-sites in the codebase. Rule-of-Three is not met.
findings.location: Selected design — Read helper section; AC line 3 (`opcert.opcert_from_record(record)`)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/test_reuse_surface_doc.py` and `docs/reuse-surface.md` to the edit set (or explicitly note that `sign_opcert_manifest` is intentionally NOT part of the documented reuse surface).
findings.finding: `tests/unit/test_reuse_surface_doc.py` is an unenumerated consumer: it locks down the signing API surface by introspecting `signing.*` parameter lists against `docs/reuse-surface.md`. If `sign_opcert_manifest` is added to `signing.py` and documented in `docs/reuse-surface.md`, this test and the doc will need updating — but neither file appears in the plan's edit set.
findings.location: Selected design — Producer bullet; tests/unit/test_reuse_surface_doc.py; docs/reuse-surface.md
findings.suggested_fix: If `sign_opcert_manifest` is an internal primitive (not a documented reuse-surface entry), state that explicitly in the plan so the implementer does not add it to `docs/reuse-surface.md`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pin the envelope serialization contract: specify that `envelope` is stored as the `str` returned by `dsse.encode(envelope.payload_type, envelope.payload, [...])` (matching the `author_sig` precedent in `_seam.py:301`), and that `opcert_from_record` reconstructs a `dsse.Envelope` via `dsse.decode(record['envelope'])` — so the producer, reducer passthrough, and read helper all interoperate correctly.
findings.finding: The plan says the producer appends a SIGNATURE event with `envelope` set to `dsse.encode(...)` output, and `process_signature` preserves `envelope` via `data.get('envelope')`. But `dsse.encode()` returns a JSON string (str), while a stored 'envelope dict' would be a parsed Python dict. The plan does not specify which format is stored in the event data, nor what format `opcert_from_record` expects to receive and reconstruct a `dsse.Envelope` from. The producer, reducer, and read helper must agree on this serialization contract; an unspecified mismatch (str vs. dict) will cause a runtime error or silent data corruption on the read path.
findings.location: Selected design — Option A, Producer bullet ('the encoded envelope (dsse.encode)'); Reducer bullet; Read helper bullet
findings.suggested_fix: In the Producer bullet, change 'the encoded envelope (dsse.encode)' to 'the encoded envelope stored as a JSON string: `envelope_str = dsse.encode(env.payload_type, env.payload, [{'keyid': s.keyid, 'sig': s.sig} for s in env.signatures])`; and `opcert_from_record` reconstructs via `dsse.decode(record['envelope'])`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the Read helper description, explicitly state that `opcert_from_record` calls `dsse.decode(record['envelope'])` to recover the envelope from the stored JSON string, co-locating the decode rule with the schema.
findings.finding: The envelope serialization decision rule (stored as JSON string via dsse.encode, not as a parsed dict) is stated only once in the producer description and is not co-located with the opcert_from_record decode protocol. An implementer reading only the Read helper section has no specification of whether to call dsse.decode(record['envelope']) or treat record['envelope'] as an already-parsed dict.
findings.location: Selected design — Producer bullet ('encoded envelope (dsse.encode)'); Read helper bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Read helper bullet: 'calls dsse.decode(record["envelope"]) to recover the Envelope from the stored JSON string (as produced by dsse.encode in the producer)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the failure/exception contract for `sign_opcert_manifest` (e.g. raises `SigningError` on ssh-keygen unavailability or event-append failure, mirroring `sign_manifest`).
findings.finding: No fallback is specified for sign_opcert_manifest when opcert.sign_opcert raises (e.g. SshKeygenUnavailable when ssh-keygen is absent, or a subprocess failure). sign_manifest handles its own failure path via SigningError; the analogous error handling protocol for sign_opcert_manifest is absent.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
